scenario: Production payment-settlement batch failing intermittently in the Singapore (SG) region.
notes: Synthetic Jira-style corpus with ground-truth structure. Seeded conditions: (1) a corroboration cluster around the connection-pool root cause and its fix RES-12; (2) contradiction pairs (firewall hypothesis vs pool-exhaustion evidence); (3) stale/superseded nodes (RUN-01, RES-08); (4) dangling references (JIRA-4300, DOC-99) that point to ids absent from the corpus; (5) restricted/hr_only nodes (RES-13, JIRA-4416, CMT-09) for the access layer.

nodes.title: Production payment-settlement batch failing intermittently in SG region
nodes.body: Critical incident. The nightly payment-settlement batch in the Singapore (SG) region is failing intermittently, leaving settlements in a pending state and breaching the regional SLA. Failures cluster around APAC business hours and appear correlated with regional database failover events. Multiple downstream teams are impacted. This is the central incident tracking the investigation and resolution.
nodes.author: k.ong

nodes.title: Settlement batch timeout in SG region
nodes.body: The settlement batch job times out intermittently in the SG region. Logs show the job hanging while acquiring database connections, then aborting after the connection-acquisition timeout. The failures coincide with SG primary-to-standby database failover. This is the canonical engineering ticket for the incident. Resolved by the failover-aware connection handling delivered in RES-12.
nodes.author: a.sharma

nodes.title: DB connection pool exhausted during SG failover
nodes.body: Root-cause ticket. During SG regional database failover the application connection pool is exhausted: connections to the old primary are not evicted and the pool saturates, so the settlement batch cannot acquire connections and times out. Pool saturation metrics confirm active connections hitting the configured maximum during failover windows. Resolved by RES-12.
nodes.author: r.gupta

nodes.title: Duplicate: SG settlement timeouts recurring nightly
nodes.body: Recurring nightly settlement timeouts reported in the SG region. After triage this was found to be the same failure as JIRA-4412 (connection-pool exhaustion on failover) and is tracked as a duplicate.
nodes.author: j.lim

nodes.title: Connection-pool metrics spike during SG failover drills
nodes.body: Observability ticket. During scheduled SG failover drills the database connection-pool utilisation spikes to 100% and connection-acquisition latency climbs sharply. This reproduces the saturation behaviour seen in production and corroborates the pool-exhaustion root cause in JIRA-4399.
nodes.author: r.gupta

nodes.title: SG batch failures caused by firewall ACL change on DB port
nodes.body: Hypothesis ticket claiming the SG settlement batch failures are caused by a firewall ACL change blocking the database port during the maintenance window, intermittently dropping connections. This hypothesis was investigated and not substantiated; it directly conflicts with the connection-pool-exhaustion evidence in JIRA-4399. Closed as wontfix after the network team found no ACL changes.
nodes.author: m.chen

nodes.title: Intermittent settlement failures only during APAC business hours
nodes.body: Pattern-analysis ticket. The settlement failures are intermittent and occur predominantly during APAC business hours when load is highest and failover drills are scheduled. This timing pattern is consistent with load-induced connection-pool saturation during failover.
nodes.author: a.sharma

nodes.title: Prior occurrence: SG settlement delays in Q4
nodes.body: Earlier occurrence of SG settlement delays observed in the previous quarter. At the time the issue was mitigated by a manual batch restart and not fully root-caused. It is a prior occurrence of the same class of failure later diagnosed as connection-pool exhaustion on failover.
nodes.author: j.lim

nodes.title: Settlement reconciliation mismatch after batch retries
nodes.body: When the settlement batch retries after a timeout, some records are reconciled twice, producing a reconciliation mismatch. This is a downstream side effect of the non-failover-aware retry behaviour and is tracked alongside the main incident.
nodes.author: m.chen

nodes.title: Batch scheduler retries are not failover-aware
nodes.body: The batch scheduler retries failed settlement runs immediately without accounting for an in-progress database failover, so retries hit the still-saturated connection pool and also fail. Making retries failover-aware is part of the fix delivered in RES-12.
nodes.author: r.gupta

nodes.title: Add monitoring alert on connection-pool saturation
nodes.body: Preventive action item. Add an alert that fires when the database connection-pool utilisation crosses a saturation threshold, so future failover-induced pool exhaustion is detected before the settlement batch fails. Tracked as a follow-up from postmortem RES-12.
nodes.author: r.gupta

nodes.title: Customer impact and SLA-credit assessment for SG settlement outage
nodes.body: Restricted commercial ticket assessing the customer-facing impact of the SG settlement outage, including affected counterparties and the SLA-credit owed. Contains confidential customer and contractual information and is restricted to leads.
nodes.author: k.ong

nodes.title: Escalation: SG settlement SLA breach
nodes.body: Escalation ticket raised because the SG settlement SLA was breached during the incident. Routes the commercial impact assessment to the restricted ticket JIRA-4416 and tracks management communication.
nodes.author: k.ong

nodes.title: Implement failover-aware connection-pool eviction
nodes.body: Fix task. Implement failover-aware eviction so stale connections to the demoted primary are dropped promptly and the pool re-establishes connections to the new primary, preventing saturation during failover. Delivered as part of RES-12.
nodes.author: a.sharma

nodes.title: Increase max connection-pool size for SG cluster
nodes.body: Fix task. Increase the maximum database connection-pool size for the SG cluster and tune acquisition timeouts to provide headroom during failover. Delivered alongside the eviction fix as part of RES-12.
nodes.author: a.sharma

nodes.title: Postmortem: SG settlement batch failure — connection-pool exhaustion on failover
nodes.body: Authoritative postmortem. Root cause: during SG regional database failover the application connection pool was not failover-aware; stale connections to the demoted primary were not evicted, the pool saturated, and the settlement batch timed out acquiring connections. Fix: failover-aware connection eviction (JIRA-4420), increased max pool size and tuned timeouts (JIRA-4421), and failover-aware scheduler retries (JIRA-4402). Verified in production with no further failures across subsequent failover events. Supersedes the earlier timeout-bump mitigation RES-08.
nodes.author: k.ong

nodes.title: Earlier mitigation: increase settlement batch timeout
nodes.body: Earlier, partial mitigation that simply increased the settlement batch's connection-acquisition timeout. This reduced the frequency of failures but did not address the underlying pool exhaustion, so failures still occurred under longer failover events. Superseded by the proper failover-aware fix in RES-12.
nodes.author: m.chen

nodes.title: Vendor RCA: managed-database failover connection-handling defect (confidential)
nodes.body: Restricted vendor root-cause analysis confirming a connection-handling defect in the managed-database failover path that leaves client connections pointing at the demoted primary. Corroborates the internal pool-exhaustion diagnosis. Contains confidential vendor contractual detail and is restricted to leads.
nodes.author: v.rao

nodes.title: Postmortem review sign-off for SG settlement incident
nodes.body: Formal review sign-off confirming the postmortem RES-12 findings and that the failover-aware fixes were validated and closed. Records the approval of the corrective and preventive actions.
nodes.author: k.ong

nodes.title: Runbook: restart settlement-batch service on hang
nodes.body: Legacy runbook instructing on-call engineers to restart the settlement-batch service whenever it hangs. This is a stale mitigation that does not address failover-induced pool exhaustion and can cause duplicate reconciliation on retry. Superseded by the failover-aware recovery runbook RUN-02.
nodes.author: j.lim

nodes.title: Runbook: SG failover-aware settlement recovery
nodes.body: Current runbook for recovering the SG settlement batch. Steps: confirm whether a database failover is in progress, allow failover-aware connection eviction to re-point the pool, verify pool utilisation has recovered, then resume the batch idempotently to avoid duplicate reconciliation. Supersedes the legacy restart-only runbook RUN-01.
nodes.author: r.gupta

nodes.title: Reference: SG region database failover architecture
nodes.body: Reference documentation describing the SG region database high-availability and failover architecture: primary/standby topology, failover triggers, and expected client reconnection behaviour. Useful background for understanding why a non-failover-aware connection pool saturates.
nodes.author: n.tan

nodes.title: Reference: settlement batch design and SLA
nodes.body: Reference documentation describing the payment-settlement batch design, its scheduling, database access patterns, and the regional SLA targets it must meet. Provides context for the impact of settlement timeouts.
nodes.author: n.tan

nodes.title: Comment on JIRA-4399: active connections hit max during failover
nodes.body: During the last failover window I watched active database connections climb to the configured maximum and stay pinned there for several minutes while the batch failed to acquire connections. This strongly supports the pool-exhaustion theory.
nodes.author: r.gupta

nodes.title: Comment on JIRA-4412: larger pool fixed it in staging
nodes.body: We increased the connection-pool size and added eviction in staging and reproduced a failover with no settlement timeouts. The fix looks effective.
nodes.author: a.sharma

nodes.title: Comment on JIRA-4408: network team confirms no ACL changes
nodes.body: The network team reviewed the change log for the maintenance window and confirmed there were no firewall ACL changes affecting the database port. This rules out the firewall hypothesis.
nodes.author: n.tan

nodes.title: Comment on JIRA-4408: I still think it's the firewall
nodes.body: I'm not convinced it's the connection pool — the timing lines up with the maintenance window and I think a firewall or network ACL change is intermittently dropping the database connections. This conflicts with the pool-exhaustion evidence reported elsewhere.
nodes.author: m.chen

nodes.title: Comment on RES-12: matches what we saw in production
nodes.body: The postmortem's root cause matches the production telemetry exactly — pool saturation during failover and recovery once eviction is in place. Agree with the conclusion.
nodes.author: j.lim

nodes.title: Comment on RES-12: tracking the action items
nodes.body: Logging the postmortem action items: ship failover-aware eviction, raise pool size, add saturation alerting, and update the recovery runbook. Owners assigned.
nodes.author: k.ong

nodes.title: Comment on JIRA-4407: failures track APAC peak load
nodes.body: Plotted the failures against load — they cluster tightly during APAC peak hours, which is also when the scheduled failover drills run. Consistent with load-plus-failover saturation.
nodes.author: a.sharma

nodes.title: Comment on JIRA-4390: same symptoms last quarter
nodes.body: These symptoms look identical to the SG settlement delays we had last quarter that we just restarted our way out of. We should have root-caused it then.
nodes.author: j.lim

nodes.title: On-call staffing gap during the incident (HR)
nodes.body: HR-restricted note recording that the on-call rotation had a staffing gap during the incident window due to approved leave, which delayed initial response. Contains personnel information and is restricted to HR.
nodes.author: h.wong

nodes.title: Comment on RUN-01: restarting no longer works
nodes.body: Heads up — just restarting the batch service per the old runbook no longer reliably recovers things after a failover and can cause duplicate reconciliation. Use the new failover-aware runbook instead.
nodes.author: r.gupta
